---
name: Adopter Feedback
about: Share your experience using OKPF in a real or experimental workflow
title: "[Adopter Feedback] "
labels: adopter-feedback
assignees: ''
---

<!-- Thank you for trying OKPF. This feedback directly shapes the project's direction. Be blunt — honest critical feedback is more useful than polite encouragement. -->

## Use case

What were you trying to do? Describe the knowledge packaging problem, not the OKPF features.

<!-- Example: "Package onboarding docs for a microservices project so new engineers can query them via RAG with source tracing." -->

---

## What alternative were you comparing against?

<!-- Examples: plain Markdown folder, YAML front matter, JSONL dataset, internal schema, Hugging Face dataset format, RO-Crate, nothing (this was a greenfield workflow) -->

---

## What worked?

What specific aspects of OKPF were useful? Be concrete.

<!-- Examples: "The manifest made it clear what license applied before ingestion." / "The JSONL records were easy to ingest without writing a custom parser." / "Provenance sources.json let us trace an answer back to a specific source doc." -->

---

## What was too complex or unnecessary?

What added overhead without clear benefit?

<!-- Examples: "The provenance sources.json format required too many fields for a simple use case." / "Required record fields were more than we needed." / "The validator error messages were hard to understand." -->

---

## Did OKPF improve attribution or provenance tracking?

Yes / No / Partially — explain what happened.

---

## Did OKPF improve ingestion reliability or reduce ambiguity?

Yes / No / Partially — explain what happened.

---

## Would you use OKPF again for this workflow?

Yes / No / Maybe — and why.

---

## What would make OKPF more useful for your use case?

---

## Context (optional)

- OKPF version / commit:
- Python version:
- What kind of downstream system consumed the pack (RAG, viewer, custom script, etc.):
- Any link to a public repo or example you can share:
